findings.suggestion: Guard every map access with the mutex. Switch to sync.RWMutex and wrap the read: `c.mu.RLock(); v, ok := c.data[key]; c.mu.RUnlock(); return v, ok`. Keep Set using Lock/Unlock. Add a `go test -race` test exercising Get and Set from multiple goroutines to prove the race is gone.
findings.summary: Get (source.go:19-22) reads c.data[key] with no lock held, while Set (source.go:25-28) writes c.data[key] under c.mu.Lock(). A lock on the write side alone provides no protection for a lockless reader. Under concurrent Get/Set the Go runtime raises `fatal error: concurrent map read and map write`, a hard crash that recover() cannot catch — the whole process dies. This is a user-facing availability regression: a cache under normal concurrent load takes the service down entirely instead of degrading gracefully.
findings.title: Data race: Get reads map without holding mu while Set writes under lock — causes non-recoverable process crash

findings.suggestion: Guard the read in Get with the same lock. Preferred: change line 10 to `mu sync.RWMutex`, wrap Get's read in `c.mu.RLock(); defer c.mu.RUnlock()`, and use `c.mu.Lock(); defer c.mu.Unlock()` in Set. Then prove it: add a test that runs Get/Set concurrently and run `go test -race ./...` (fails now, passes after) plus `go vet ./...`.
findings.summary: source.go:20 reads c.data[key] in Get() with no lock, while Set() (lines 26-28) writes c.data under c.mu.Lock(). A concurrent map read racing a map write is undefined behavior in Go; the runtime's map-race check aborts the process with 'fatal error: concurrent map read and map write'. The type comment (line 18) states Get is called from many goroutines, so this is the expected path, not an edge case.
findings.title: Data race: Cache.Get reads shared map without holding the mutex

findings.suggestion: Acquire the lock in Get (c.mu.Lock(); defer c.mu.Unlock() around the read), or change mu to sync.RWMutex and use RLock/RUnlock in Get to retain read concurrency. Both accessors must respect the same lock for it to be effective.
findings.summary: source.go:20 reads c.data[key] with no lock held, while Set writes c.data at source.go:27 under c.mu (lines 26-28). The mu field (source.go:10) establishes the invariant that data is only accessed under the lock; Set enforces it but Get violates it, leaving the invariant half-enforced. A concurrent map read (line 20) racing a map write (line 27) is undefined behavior in Go and triggers the runtime's fatal error 'concurrent map read and map write', crashing the process; a locked writer does not protect against an unlocked reader. The doc comment at line 18 confirms Get is 'called from many goroutines'.
findings.title: Get reads shared map without holding mu — data race with Set

summary:
Risk-tiered review (lite) completed.

New concurrent cache with a genuine data race: Get (line 20) reads the map without the lock that Set (lines 26-28) uses to write it. This is an unrecoverable fatal error under concurrent use in Go, not a soft bug. The struct's mu field declares the 'access data only under lock' invariant, which Set enforces and Get breaks — a classic half-enforced-invariant defect. Currently 0 callers (new file), so no live impact yet, hence high rather than critical. Verification via `go test -race` could not be run in this session; finding rests on Go's documented map semantics.

Single new file implementing a cache with a deliberate, self-documented data race: Get reads the shared map without holding the mutex that Set uses for writes. Concurrent Get/Set triggers Go's `fatal error: concurrent map read and map write`, a non-recoverable process crash — a critical availability/product-safety defect the moment this is used on any concurrent path. Fix is to hold the lock (RWMutex) around all map accesses. Blast radius is currently 0 callers (latent), and no -race test was run (verification gate still open), but the language semantics make the defect certain.

Single-file cache package with a genuine, crash-capable data race. Get() (source.go:20) reads the shared map with no synchronization while Set() writes under c.mu — Go treats concurrent map read/write as a fatal runtime error, so this can hard-crash any concurrent user. The comments correctly self-identify the bug; the code still ships it. One high-severity blocker; no other issues in the 29 changed lines.
